food_name: Cháo Lươn Nghệ An (Vietnamese Eel Congee from Nghệ An)
food_subgroup: Rice porridge / congee with fish and seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee class (FOODON_03302051) but no specific entry for Vietnamese eel congee. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice porridge as a category. No specific code exists for Vietnamese eel congee; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Vietnamese eel congee or eel porridge as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: seasoning with fish sauce and spices

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Cháo Lươn Nghệ An is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as served in Vietnamese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional composition of rice congee with eel, estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked rice porridge and freshwater eel, combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese congee dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Cháo Lươn Nghệ An exists in any approved primary database. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked rice porridge (FDC ID 168882) and cooked freshwater eel (FDC ID 175168), weighted at approximately 65% rice porridge base and 35% eel by mass, with minor adjustments for fish sauce, herbs, and seasoning. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese congee dishes used as a cross-reference for plausibility. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice porridge / congee analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cháo Lươn Nghệ An. GI of 57 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice porridge / congee (GI approximately 57, medium category), which is the closest analogous food. The addition of eel protein and fat is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain congee, so 57 represents a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (57 × 8.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 16.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing roughly 47% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from rice porridge base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 57 and a glycemic load of 16.3 per standard bowl, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from freshwater eel and low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Freshwater eel contributes beneficial unsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s; however, the moderate sodium content from fish sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are also managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cháo Lươn Nghệ An is a traditional Vietnamese rice congee originating from Nghệ An Province in north-central Vietnam. It is made by slow-simmering white rice in water or light broth until the grains break down into a smooth, thick porridge, then topping or mixing in freshwater eel (lươn) that has been separately stir-fried or sautéed with turmeric, lemongrass, shallots, ginger, and fish sauce. The dish is characteristically fragrant and savoury, with a silky congee base contrasting with tender, lightly spiced eel pieces. It is garnished with fresh Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), spring onion, and cracked black pepper. Nghệ An-style preparation is distinguished by its bold use of turmeric and lemongrass, giving the eel a golden colour and aromatic depth not found in congee versions from other Vietnamese regions. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from eel (approximately 5.8 g per 100 g), beneficial unsaturated fats including omega-3 fatty acids, and a moderate glycemic index of approximately 57 due to the rice porridge base. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light meal in Vietnam and is available in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, where it is served as a specialty regional dish.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Cháo Lươn Nghệ An exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked rice porridge and cooked freshwater eel, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese congee dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice porridge as the closest analogous food. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (cooked rice porridge FDC 168882 and cooked freshwater eel FDC 175168) weighted at approximately 65:35 rice-to-eel ratio, with minor adjustments for fish sauce and herbs. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database rice porridge entry; actual GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat from eel. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in both rice porridge and freshwater eel per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical fish sauce seasoning in Vietnamese congee; actual sodium will vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 5. The cuisine field is set to 'Vietnamese' rather than 'Singapore' as this dish is a Vietnamese regional specialty served in Singapore by a Vietnamese restaurant; the input record incorrectly classified it as 'Singapore' cuisine. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients and are not independently verified for this specific dish preparation. 7. Multilingual names for Tamil and Hindi are transliterations of the Vietnamese dish name into the respective scripts, as no established native-language names exist for this Vietnamese regional dish in Tamil or Hindi culinary traditions; these are provided to satisfy the Singapore multilingual requirement given the restaurant context.
